---
title: "The Workhorse Tests: z, t, Chi-Square, and F"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240714)

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # the test statistic / data
NULL   = "#c1121f"     # the null distribution / rejection region
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / observed value
GREY   = "#6c757d"

def normal_pdf(t, mu=0.0, sigma=1.0):
    """The standard Gaussian density — the null curve for the z-test."""
    return np.exp(-0.5 * ((t - mu) / sigma) ** 2) / (sigma * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second article in the **Hypothesis
> Testing** movement and the place where the abstract logic of testing becomes the four
> tests you will actually run. It assumes [INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*],
> which built the decision-theoretic frame — null vs. alternative, the significance level
> $\alpha$, Type I / Type II error, **power**, the **p-value** as a tail probability under
> the null (and the long list of what it is *not*), and the **duality** between a test and
> a confidence interval. It assumes [INFR-C2 *the t-Distribution and Small-Sample
> Intervals*], which built from scratch the two distributions this article turns into
> decisions: the **chi-square distribution of the sample variance**
> ($(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n-1}$, with $\bar X_n$ and $s^2$ independent) and the
> **Student $t$** as a Normal over a scaled independent chi-square root. It leans on
> [INFR-A2 *LLN and the CLT*] for every null distribution that is only *approximately*
> Normal, and on [INFR-A1] for the sampling-distribution and standard-error vocabulary.
>
> This article **sets up** [INFR-D3 *The Asymptotic Trinity*] — the likelihood-ratio,
> Wald, and score tests, of which the chi-square tests here are the simplest special cases
> — and [INFR-D5 *Multiple Comparisons*], which asks what happens when you run many of
> these tests at once. It is consumed directly by **Regression**, whose **F-test for
> nested models** is the F-test derived here applied to a fitted linear model.
>
> It imports nothing above the Calculus-I floor that the prerequisites have not already
> built: the chi-square and $t$ densities, the additivity of independent chi-squares, and
> the CLT all arrive by their statements from [INFR-C2] and [INFR-A2], flagged at the point
> of use. The **F-distribution** — a ratio of two independent scaled chi-squares — is
> assembled here in full.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

For the everyday one- and two-sample questions — *is this mean what we claim? do these two
groups differ? do these counts match a model? is this variance larger than that one?* —
statistics offers four reusable test statistics, each with a known distribution **under the
null hypothesis**: the **z**, the **t**, the **chi-square**, and the **F**. This article
shows they are not four unrelated recipes but four faces of one idea. Every one is a
*standardized discrepancy*: how far the data sit from what the null predicts, measured in
units of the noise the null itself implies. The **z** and **t** standardize a mean (the $t$
paying for an estimated $\sigma$, exactly the [INFR-C2] pivot now read as a decision); the
**chi-square** standardizes a *vector* of squared discrepancies (counts versus expected
counts, or a sample variance versus a claimed one); and the **F** is a *ratio of two
independent chi-squares*, which we derive from the ground up, used to compare two variances
and — its most important application — two nested models. We carry each through a real
worked example with the rejection region drawn, we make the test/interval duality concrete,
and we close at the edge: which test survives which assumption violation (non-normality,
unequal variance, dependence, sparse counts) and which collapses, so the reader leaves able
to *choose the right workhorse and know when none of them is honest.* The single claim the
article defends: **the choice of test is the choice of how to measure "surprise," and once
you can read each statistic as a standardized discrepancy you can derive the right one
rather than memorize a flowchart.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A hypothesis test reduces to one number: a **test statistic** that measures how far the
data fall from what the null hypothesis predicts, scaled by the noise the null itself
implies — a *standardized discrepancy*. The four workhorses differ only in what is being
standardized and how the noise is estimated:

- **z** $=\dfrac{\hat\theta-\theta_0}{\sigma_0/\sqrt n}$ — a mean against a claim, with the
  noise scale **known**. Null distribution: $\mathcal N(0,1)$.
- **t** $=\dfrac{\hat\theta-\theta_0}{s/\sqrt n}$ — the same, with the noise scale
  **estimated** from the sample. Null distribution: $t_{\nu}$, the $z$'s heavier-tailed
  sibling that pays for not knowing $\sigma$.
- **$\chi^2$** $=\sum\dfrac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ (counts) or $\dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2}$
  (variance) — a *sum* of squared standardized discrepancies. Null distribution:
  $\chi^2_{\nu}$.
- **F** $=\dfrac{\chi^2_{d_1}/d_1}{\chi^2_{d_2}/d_2}$ — a **ratio** of two independent
  scaled chi-squares, comparing two variances or two nested models. Null distribution:
  $F_{d_1,d_2}$.

In every case you compute the statistic, locate it on its **null distribution**, and read
off the **p-value** — the probability the null would produce a discrepancy at least this
extreme. Small p-value, the data are surprising under the null, reject it. The whole art is
choosing the statistic whose null distribution you can actually write down.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. By the end the four statistics should feel like one
template — *(observed $-$ expected)/(scale)*, squared and summed when there are many
coordinates, ratioed when two independent variance estimates are being compared — rather
than four entries in a table you memorized the night before an exam.

# Why we are doing this: from "is it surprising?" to a number {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the entire article: **how do we convert a vague feeling that
the data disagree with a claim into a single number whose behavior-under-the-claim we know
exactly?** Every statistic below is an answer to a specific version of that question, and
every choice in its construction traces back to *which* discrepancy we are measuring and
*what noise* the null says should surround it.

## What [INFR-D1] gave us {#sec-d1-recap}

[INFR-D1] built the machine but left the gears unspecified. It said: fix a **null
hypothesis** $H_0$ (a precise claim about the population, e.g. $\mu=\mu_0$), pick a **test
statistic** $T$ whose distribution *under $H_0$* is known, observe its value $t_{\text{obs}}$,
and report the **p-value**

$$
p \;=\; P_{H_0}\bigl(T \text{ at least as extreme as } t_{\text{obs}}\bigr),
$$ {#eq-pvalue}

the probability that the null — *if true* — would generate a discrepancy at least as large
as the one we saw. A small $p$ means the data are improbable under $H_0$, which we read as
evidence against $H_0$. [INFR-D1] also nailed down what $p$ is **not**: not the probability
$H_0$ is true, not the probability the result replicates, not an effect size. And it proved
the **duality**: rejecting $H_0:\theta=\theta_0$ at level $\alpha$ is exactly equivalent to
$\theta_0$ falling *outside* the $1-\alpha$ confidence interval for $\theta$.

What [INFR-D1] did *not* do is tell you which $T$ to use for a given question, or what its
null distribution actually is. That is the entire job of this article. The phrase "whose
distribution under $H_0$ is known" is doing enormous work, and the four workhorses are
precisely the four cases where we *can* write that distribution down in closed form.

## The unifying idea: a standardized discrepancy {#sec-unify}

Here is the thread that ties all four together, stated once so every derivation below is an
instance of it rather than a fresh invention. A test statistic is built in three moves:

1. **Discrepancy.** Form *observed minus expected-under-the-null*: $\hat\theta-\theta_0$, or
   for counts $O_i - E_i$, or for a variance $s^2 - \sigma_0^2$. This is zero on average
   when $H_0$ holds and grows as the data pull away from the claim.
2. **Standardize.** Divide by the *scale of the noise the null predicts* — the standard
   error of that discrepancy. This makes the statistic **scale-free**: it no longer matters
   whether we measured in grams or kilograms, only how many "noise units" away we landed.
3. **Aggregate, if there are many coordinates.** A single discrepancy, standardized, is a
   $z$ (Normal) or a $t$ (Normal with estimated scale). *Many* squared standardized
   discrepancies summed is a **chi-square**. A *ratio* of two such aggregated noise
   estimates is an **F**.

That is the whole taxonomy. The reason there are exactly these four and not forty is that
these are the combinations whose distribution under the null is exactly (or, via the CLT,
approximately) one of three named curves — Normal, chi-square, $t$ — plus their ratio, F.
@fig-family-map draws the genealogy.

```{python}
#| label: fig-family-map
#| fig-cap: 'The genealogy of the workhorse tests, all descended from the standard Normal. A single standardized discrepancy with **known** scale is $z\sim N(0,1)$; with **estimated** scale it is $t$ (a Normal over a scaled chi-square root, [INFR-C2]). The sum of $k$ squared independent standard Normals is $\chi^2_k$ — the statistic for counts and for a variance. The ratio of two independent scaled chi-squares is $F$ — the statistic for comparing two variances or two nested models. Each arrow is a construction this article carries out; nothing here is asserted.'
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.4))
ax.axis("off")

def box(x, y, text, color=DATA, w=2.6, h=0.9):
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((x - w/2, y - h/2), w, h, fill=True,
                               facecolor="white", edgecolor=color, lw=2.0, zorder=2))
    ax.text(x, y, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10, zorder=3)

box(0, 4, "$Z \\sim N(0,1)$\n(known scale)", color=DATA)
box(-3.2, 2, "$t_k = \\dfrac{Z}{\\sqrt{Q_k/k}}$\n(estimated scale)", color=ACCENT)
box(0, 2, "$\\chi^2_k = Z_1^2+\\cdots+Z_k^2$\n(sum of squares)", color=NULL)
box(3.4, 2, "$F_{d_1,d_2}=\\dfrac{\\chi^2_{d_1}/d_1}{\\chi^2_{d_2}/d_2}$\n(ratio)", color=GREY)

ax.annotate("", xy=(-3.0, 2.5), xytext=(-0.6, 3.6),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=ACCENT, lw=1.6))
ax.annotate("", xy=(0, 2.5), xytext=(0, 3.55),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=NULL, lw=1.6))
ax.annotate("", xy=(2.6, 2.5), xytext=(0.9, 2.5),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=GREY, lw=1.6))
ax.text(-2.2, 3.2, "divide by\n$\\sqrt{\\chi^2/k}$", fontsize=8, color=ACCENT, ha="center")
ax.text(0.25, 3.05, "square\n& sum", fontsize=8, color=NULL, ha="left")
ax.text(1.75, 2.75, "ratio of two", fontsize=8, color=GREY, ha="center")
ax.set_xlim(-5, 5.2); ax.set_ylim(1.2, 4.7)
ax.set_title("Four workhorses, one ancestor")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why do we standardize by the null's noise rather than the observed noise?"* Because the
p-value @eq-pvalue is computed **assuming the null is true** — it asks how surprising the
data are *in the null's world*. The denominator must therefore be the spread the null
predicts, so that under $H_0$ the statistic has a known, parameter-free distribution (a
**pivot**, in the [INFR-C1] sense). If we standardized by something the alternative
implies, the statistic's null distribution would depend on unknowns and we could not
compute a p-value at all. This is the same pivotal logic as confidence intervals, run in the
other direction: there we trapped $\theta$; here we fix $\theta=\theta_0$ and ask whether
the data are consistent with it.
:::

# The z-test: a mean against a claim, scale known {#sec-z}

Start with the simplest workhorse, because it sets the template the other three decorate.

## Setup and statistic {#sec-z-setup}

We have $X_1,\dots,X_n$ i.i.d. with unknown mean $\mu$ and **known** standard deviation
$\sigma$. (Known $\sigma$ is the classroom fiction [INFR-C2] warned about; we keep it
briefly because it is the cleanest case, and it is genuinely realized in a few settings —
see the worked example.) We wish to test

$$
H_0:\ \mu=\mu_0 \qquad\text{versus}\qquad H_1:\ \mu\neq\mu_0
$$

(a **two-sided** alternative). Follow the three moves of @sec-unify. The discrepancy is
$\bar X_n-\mu_0$. Its standard error under $H_0$ is $\sigma/\sqrt n$, because [INFR-A1]
established $\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)=\sigma^2/n$ and $H_0$ fixes the center at
$\mu_0$. Standardizing,

$$
Z \;=\; \frac{\bar X_n-\mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n}.
$$ {#eq-z-stat}

Under $H_0$, what is the distribution of $Z$? If the $X_i$ are themselves Normal, then
$\bar X_n\sim\mathcal N(\mu_0,\sigma^2/n)$ *exactly*, so $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ exactly. If
the $X_i$ are not Normal, the **CLT** [INFR-A2] says $\bar X_n$ is approximately
$\mathcal N(\mu_0,\sigma^2/n)$ for large $n$, so $Z\stackrel{\cdot}{\sim}\mathcal N(0,1)$
approximately. Either way the **null distribution is the standard Normal** — a curve we know
to the last decimal.

## From statistic to decision {#sec-z-decision}

Now read the p-value off that curve. Because the alternative is two-sided, "at least as
extreme as $z_{\text{obs}}$" means $|Z|\ge|z_{\text{obs}}|$ — a discrepancy of that size in
*either* direction. So

$$
p \;=\; P_{H_0}\bigl(|Z|\ge |z_{\text{obs}}|\bigr)
\;=\; 2\bigl(1-\Phi(|z_{\text{obs}}|)\bigr),
$$ {#eq-z-pvalue}

where $\Phi$ is the standard-Normal CDF and the factor $2$ collects both tails (by
symmetry each tail beyond $|z_{\text{obs}}|$ has probability $1-\Phi(|z_{\text{obs}}|)$).
Equivalently — the **rejection-region** view — fix a level $\alpha$ and reject $H_0$ when
$|z_{\text{obs}}|\ge z_{\alpha/2}$, the value cutting off probability $\alpha/2$ in each
tail. The two views agree exactly: $p\le\alpha \iff |z_{\text{obs}}|\ge z_{\alpha/2}$.
@fig-z-test draws both.

```{python}
#| label: fig-z-test
#| fig-cap: 'The two-sided z-test as a picture. The null distribution of $Z$ is $N(0,1)$. The red shaded tails beyond $\pm z_{0.025}=\pm 1.96$ are the level-$\alpha=0.05$ rejection region (total area 0.05). The observed statistic $z_{\text{obs}}$ (green line) lands inside or outside that region; the p-value is the total area in both tails beyond $\pm|z_{\text{obs}}|$ (green hatched), here smaller than the 0.05 rejection area (the printed value is $p=0.0164$), so we reject. Rejection region and p-value are two readings of the same Normal curve.'
z = np.linspace(-4, 4, 600)
zobs = 2.4
zc = stats.norm.ppf(0.975)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(z, normal_pdf(z), color=DATA, lw=2)
# rejection region
zr = z[np.abs(z) >= zc]
ax.fill_between(zr, normal_pdf(zr), color=NULL, alpha=0.30,
                label=fr"rejection region $|Z|\geq{zc:.2f}$ (area 0.05)")
# p-value tails
zp = z[np.abs(z) >= zobs]
ax.fill_between(zp, normal_pdf(zp), color=ACCENT, alpha=0.45, hatch="///",
                label=fr"p-value tails $|Z|\geq{zobs:.1f}$")
ax.axvline(zobs, color=ACCENT, lw=2)
ax.axvline(-zobs, color=ACCENT, lw=2, ls=":")
ax.text(zobs + 0.05, 0.34, r"$z_{\mathrm{obs}}$", color=ACCENT)
ax.set_xlabel("$Z$"); ax.set_ylabel("density under $H_0$")
ax.set_title("Two-sided z-test: rejection region and p-value")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
pval = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(zobs))
print(f"z_obs = {zobs},  two-sided p = 2(1-Phi(z_obs)) = {pval:.4f}")
print(f"critical z_(0.025) = {zc:.4f};  reject since {zobs} > {zc:.4f}")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"One-sided or two-sided — how do I choose, and what changes?"* Choose the alternative
**before** seeing the data, from the scientific question. A two-sided $H_1:\mu\ne\mu_0$
splits $\alpha$ across both tails ($z_{\alpha/2}=1.96$ at $\alpha=0.05$). A one-sided
$H_1:\mu>\mu_0$ puts all of $\alpha$ in the upper tail ($z_\alpha=1.645$), giving more power
to detect an increase — at the cost of *zero* power to detect a decrease, which it will
never flag no matter how large. The honest danger: picking the side *after* seeing which
way the data went doubles your true Type I rate while reporting the one-sided p-value. If
you cannot pre-commit to a direction, use two-sided. Most reported tests are two-sided for
exactly this reason.
:::

# The t-test: the same test with the scale estimated {#sec-t}

The z-test is honest only when $\sigma$ is known, which it almost never is. Replace it with
the sample standard deviation $s$ and the statistic becomes the **t-statistic** — and this
is not a new idea, it is the [INFR-C2] pivot read as a decision.

## One-sample t {#sec-t-one}

With $\sigma$ unknown, estimate it by $s^2=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar X_n)^2$ and form

$$
T \;=\; \frac{\bar X_n-\mu_0}{s/\sqrt n}.
$$ {#eq-t-stat}

[INFR-C2] proved the load-bearing fact: when the $X_i$ are Normal, $T$ does **not** follow
$\mathcal N(0,1)$ — it follows **Student's $t$ with $n-1$ degrees of freedom**, because the
denominator $s$ is itself random. We do not re-derive it; we *use* it. The construction was
$T = Z/\sqrt{Q/(n-1)}$ with $Z=(\bar X_n-\mu_0)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and
$Q=(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n-1}$ independent — and the unknown $\sigma$ cancels, making
$T$ a pivot with null distribution $t_{n-1}$. The decision is identical to the z-test with
the $t$ quantile replacing the Normal one:

$$
p = 2\,P\bigl(t_{n-1}\ge|t_{\text{obs}}|\bigr),
\qquad\text{reject when }|t_{\text{obs}}|\ge t_{n-1,\,\alpha/2}.
$$ {#eq-t-pvalue}

The duality with the [INFR-C2] interval is now exact and worth saying out loud: rejecting
$H_0:\mu=\mu_0$ at level $\alpha$ happens **precisely when** $\mu_0$ falls outside
$\bar X_n\pm t_{n-1,\alpha/2}\,s/\sqrt n$. The paired interval of [INFR-C2] that "excluded
zero" was, silently, a paired $t$-test. @fig-t-vs-z shows why using the $t$ curve matters:
its heavier tails push the critical value out, so the rejection region is *wider* (harder to
reject) — the correction that stops the small-sample overconfidence [INFR-C2] measured.

```{python}
#| label: fig-t-vs-z
#| fig-cap: 'Why the t-test rejects more cautiously than the z-test at small $n$. Both null curves are centered and symmetric, but $t_4$ (blue, $n=5$) has heavier tails than $N(0,1)$ (red dashed). The two-sided 5% critical value moves out from $z=1.96$ to $t_{4,0.025}=2.78$ (vertical lines): the same data must land further out to reject. The gap is the price of estimating $\sigma$, and it shrinks to nothing as $n$ grows (by $n=30$ the curves nearly coincide).'
x = np.linspace(-5, 5, 600)
k = 4
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, stats.t.pdf(x, df=k), color=DATA, lw=2, label=fr"$t_{{{k}}}$ (null for $n={k+1}$)")
ax.plot(x, normal_pdf(x), color=NULL, ls="--", lw=1.8, label="$N(0,1)$")
tc = stats.t.ppf(0.975, df=k)
zc = stats.norm.ppf(0.975)
ax.axvline(tc, color=DATA, lw=1.5); ax.axvline(-tc, color=DATA, lw=1.5)
ax.axvline(zc, color=NULL, ls="--", lw=1.3); ax.axvline(-zc, color=NULL, ls="--", lw=1.3)
ax.text(tc + 0.05, 0.30, fr"$t_{{4,.025}}={tc:.2f}$", color=DATA, fontsize=9)
ax.text(zc + 0.05, 0.20, fr"$z_{{.025}}={zc:.2f}$", color=NULL, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlim(-5, 5); ax.set_xlabel("statistic"); ax.set_ylabel("density under $H_0$")
ax.set_title("Estimating $\\sigma$ widens the rejection region")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Two-sample t {#sec-t-two}

The most common real test is "do two groups differ in mean?" Given independent samples
$X_1,\dots,X_{n_1}\sim\mathcal N(\mu_1,\sigma_1^2)$ and
$Y_1,\dots,Y_{n_2}\sim\mathcal N(\mu_2,\sigma_2^2)$, test $H_0:\mu_1=\mu_2$, i.e.
$\mu_1-\mu_2=0$. [INFR-C2] already built both pivots; turning them into tests is immediate.

The discrepancy is $\bar X-\bar Y$ (which is $0$ on average under $H_0$). The two variants
differ only in how the denominator estimates its noise:

- **Pooled** (assumes $\sigma_1=\sigma_2$): with
  $s_p^2=\frac{(n_1-1)s_1^2+(n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}$,

  $$
  T = \frac{\bar X-\bar Y}{s_p\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{n_1}+\tfrac{1}{n_2}}}
  \;\sim\; t_{\,n_1+n_2-2}\quad\text{under }H_0.
  $$ {#eq-pooled-t-test}

  [INFR-C2] derived the degrees of freedom by chi-square additivity:
  $(n_1-1)s_1^2/\sigma^2$ and $(n_2-1)s_2^2/\sigma^2$ are independent
  $\chi^2_{n_1-1}$ and $\chi^2_{n_2-1}$, and *degrees of freedom add when independent
  chi-squares add*, giving $\chi^2_{n_1+n_2-2}$.

- **Welch** (does not assume equal variances): with denominator
  $\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1+s_2^2/n_2}$ and the **Welch–Satterthwaite** degrees of freedom $\nu$ from
  [INFR-C2], $T\approx t_\nu$ — *approximately*, since a weighted sum of unequal-scale
  chi-squares is not exactly a chi-square.

The decision rule is @eq-t-pvalue with the appropriate degrees of freedom. We will run the
two-sample $t$ on real data in @sec-ex-ttest. The practitioner default, argued at length in
[INFR-C2], is **Welch**: it is robust to unequal variances and reduces to the pooled answer
when they happen to match.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the relationship between the two-sample t-test and a regression with one binary
predictor?"* They are the **same test**. Regress the pooled outcome on a $0/1$ group
indicator; the fitted slope is exactly $\bar Y-\bar X$, and the $t$-statistic for that slope
being zero is *identical* to the pooled two-sample $t$-statistic @eq-pooled-t-test, on the
same $n_1+n_2-2$ degrees of freedom. This is the first hint of a theme that runs through the
rest of Inference and all of Regression: **the workhorse tests are special cases of testing
coefficients in a linear model**, and the F-test of @sec-f is the general engine. Knowing
this connection is what separates "I memorized four tests" from "I understand testing."
:::

# Chi-square tests: standardizing a whole vector of discrepancies {#sec-chisq}

The $z$ and $t$ standardize *one* discrepancy. The chi-square statistic standardizes
*many at once* and sums them — its natural home is **counts** (does a frequency table match
a model?) and, separately, a **single variance** against a claimed value.

## Goodness-of-fit: do counts match expected proportions? {#sec-gof}

We observe counts $O_1,\dots,O_k$ in $k$ categories from $n=\sum_i O_i$ trials, and a null
hypothesis specifying the category probabilities $p_1,\dots,p_k$ (with $\sum p_i=1$). Under
$H_0$ the **expected count** in cell $i$ is $E_i=np_i$. Pearson's goodness-of-fit statistic
is

$$
X^2 \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{(O_i-E_i)^2}{E_i}.
$$ {#eq-pearson}

Read it as the three moves of @sec-unify: the discrepancy is $O_i-E_i$, and dividing by
$E_i$ standardizes it — *why $E_i$ and not, say, $\sqrt{E_i}$?* Because the count in a cell
is (approximately) **Poisson/Binomial**, whose variance equals its mean: a $\text{Binomial}
(n,p_i)$ count has variance $np_i(1-p_i)\approx np_i=E_i$ when $p_i$ is small, and even in
general the leading scale is $E_i$. So $(O_i-E_i)/\sqrt{E_i}$ is a discrepancy in units of
its own standard deviation — a standardized residual — and squaring and summing gives
@eq-pearson. We can make this precise.

### Why the null distribution is chi-square {#sec-gof-chisq}

We show $X^2\stackrel{\cdot}{\sim}\chi^2_{k-1}$ under $H_0$, deriving the degrees of freedom
rather than asserting them. Consider the simplest case $k=2$ first (e.g. a coin): then
$O_1=Y$, $O_2=n-Y$ with $Y\sim\text{Binomial}(n,p_1)$, $E_1=np_1$, $E_2=np_2=n(1-p_1)$.
Expand @eq-pearson:

$$
X^2 = \frac{(Y-np_1)^2}{np_1} + \frac{\bigl((n-Y)-n p_2\bigr)^2}{np_2}.
$$

Note $(n-Y)-np_2 = n(1-p_2)-Y = np_1 - Y = -(Y-np_1)$, so the **same** squared quantity
$(Y-np_1)^2$ sits in both terms. Factor it out and combine the fractions over the common
content, using $p_1+p_2=1$:

$$
X^2 = (Y-np_1)^2\Bigl(\frac{1}{np_1}+\frac{1}{np_2}\Bigr)
= (Y-np_1)^2\cdot\frac{p_1+p_2}{np_1p_2}
= \frac{(Y-np_1)^2}{np_1p_2}
= \frac{(Y-np_1)^2}{np_1(1-p_1)}.
$$

But $np_1(1-p_1)$ is exactly $\operatorname{Var}(Y)$, and $np_1=\mathbb E[Y]$, so

$$
X^2 = \left(\frac{Y-\mathbb E[Y]}{\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(Y)}}\right)^2 = Z^2,
$$

the square of a standardized Binomial count. By the **CLT** [INFR-A2], $Z\to\mathcal N(0,1)$,
so $X^2=Z^2\to\chi^2_1$ — a single squared standard Normal is $\chi^2_1$ by the [INFR-C2]
definition. For $k=2$ categories, $X^2\sim\chi^2_1$, i.e. $\chi^2_{k-1}$ with $k=2$.

The general $k$ follows the same pattern but needs a vector CLT, which we flag as imported
from [INFR-A2]'s multivariate cousin: the vector of standardized counts
$(O_i-E_i)/\sqrt{E_i}$ converges to a multivariate Normal, but the $k$ coordinates are
**not** free — they satisfy the one linear constraint $\sum_i(O_i-E_i)=\sum_i O_i-\sum_i E_i
= n-n = 0$ (the counts must total $n$). One linear constraint removes one degree of freedom,
exactly as the $\sum(X_i-\bar X_n)=0$ constraint removed one in [INFR-C2]'s variance
derivation. So $X^2$ is a sum of squares living in a $(k-1)$-dimensional space, giving

$$
X^2 \;\xrightarrow{\;d\;}\; \chi^2_{\,k-1}\quad\text{under }H_0.
$$ {#eq-gof-null}

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If you also estimated some parameters to get the expected counts, what happens to the
degrees of freedom?"* You lose one more degree of freedom **per independent parameter
estimated** from the same data. The rule is $df = k - 1 - m$, where $m$ is the number of
free parameters fit to obtain the $E_i$. The intuition is the same constraint-counting: each
estimated parameter is another equation the fitted cell probabilities must satisfy, pinning
down one more direction. Forgetting this — using $k-1$ when you fit, say, a Poisson rate to
the data — makes the test **anti-conservative** (too eager to reject), because you compare
to a chi-square with too many degrees of freedom and an inflated critical value. This is a
classic interview trap.
:::

## Test of independence: do two categorical variables relate? {#sec-independence}

The same statistic tests whether two categorical variables are **independent** in a
contingency table. Cross-classify $n$ subjects by row variable ($r$ levels) and column
variable ($c$ levels), giving observed counts $O_{ij}$. Under $H_0:$ *row and column are
independent*, the cell probability factors: $p_{ij}=p_{i\cdot}\,p_{\cdot j}$ (the product of
the marginals). We estimate the marginals from the data — row total $R_i=\sum_j O_{ij}$,
column total $C_j=\sum_i O_{ij}$ — giving expected counts

$$
E_{ij} = n\,\hat p_{i\cdot}\,\hat p_{\cdot j}
= n\cdot\frac{R_i}{n}\cdot\frac{C_j}{n} = \frac{R_i\,C_j}{n},
$$ {#eq-expected-indep}

the familiar **row total times column total over grand total**. The statistic is the same
sum of standardized squared discrepancies, now over the $rc$ cells:

$$
X^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c}\frac{(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}.
$$ {#eq-chisq-indep}

The degrees of freedom are again constraint-counting, and they come out as

$$
df = (r-1)(c-1).
$$ {#eq-df-indep}

Here is the derivation, not the assertion. The table has $rc$ cells. The grand total is
fixed ($\sum O_{ij}=n$): one constraint. To get the $E_{ij}$ we estimated the row marginals
and column marginals, but these are not all free — the $r$ row probabilities sum to $1$ (so
$r-1$ free) and the $c$ column probabilities sum to $1$ (so $c-1$ free). Starting from
$rc-1$ degrees of freedom after fixing the total, we subtract $(r-1)$ for the estimated row
marginals and $(c-1)$ for the estimated column marginals:

$$
df = (rc-1) - (r-1) - (c-1) = rc - r - c + 1 = (r-1)(c-1).
$$

The last step is just factoring: $rc-r-c+1=(r-1)(c-1)$. We will run this on a real table in
@sec-ex-chisq.

## A variance against a claim {#sec-var-test}

The chi-square also tests a *single variance*: $H_0:\sigma^2=\sigma_0^2$. [INFR-C2] handed
us the pivot directly — under Normal data, $(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n-1}$ — so under
$H_0$ the statistic is

$$
X^2 = \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2} \;\sim\; \chi^2_{n-1}.
$$ {#eq-var-test}

This is a *one-coordinate* chi-square (the variance estimate), in contrast to the
many-coordinate count statistics above; the common thread is that the null distribution is a
chi-square because the underlying quantity is a sum of squared Normals. Unlike the $z$/$t$
tests, this one is **two-tailed on an asymmetric distribution**: a sample variance can be
surprisingly *small* or surprisingly *large*, and the chi-square is right-skewed, so the two
critical values $\chi^2_{n-1,\,1-\alpha/2}$ and $\chi^2_{n-1,\,\alpha/2}$ are not mirror
images. We flag this test as the **most fragile** workhorse — it is exquisitely sensitive to
non-Normality, the subject of @sec-failure.

# The F-test: comparing two sources of variation {#sec-f}

The fourth workhorse compares **two variances** — or, far more importantly, two **nested
models** — by taking the *ratio* of two independent chi-squares. We build the F-distribution
from scratch, because it has not appeared in the track yet.

## The F-distribution, derived {#sec-f-dist}

::: {#def-f}
**Definition (F-distribution).** If $U\sim\chi^2_{d_1}$ and $V\sim\chi^2_{d_2}$ are
**independent**, then
$$
F \;=\; \frac{U/d_1}{V/d_2}
$$
follows the **F-distribution with $d_1$ numerator and $d_2$ denominator degrees of
freedom**, written $F\sim F_{d_1,d_2}$.
:::

The "$/d$" on each chi-square turns it into a *mean square* — a chi-square divided by its
degrees of freedom has expectation $1$ (since $\mathbb E[\chi^2_d]=d$, shown in [INFR-C2]).
So $F$ is a ratio of two quantities each centered near $1$ under the null: **$F$ near $1$
means the two variance estimates agree; $F$ far from $1$ means they disagree.** That is the
entire intuition.

We derive the density, every step, the same way [INFR-C2] derived the $t$ density — joint
density via change of variables, then integrate out the nuisance variable. Let
$u\ge0,v\ge0$ and recall the $\chi^2_d$ density from [INFR-C2],
$f_{\chi^2_d}(w)=\frac{1}{2^{d/2}\Gamma(d/2)}w^{d/2-1}e^{-w/2}$. Because $U,V$ are
independent, their joint density factors:

$$
f_{U,V}(u,v) = \frac{u^{d_1/2-1}e^{-u/2}}{2^{d_1/2}\Gamma(d_1/2)}
\cdot \frac{v^{d_2/2-1}e^{-v/2}}{2^{d_2/2}\Gamma(d_2/2)}.
$$

Change variables to $F=\frac{U/d_1}{V/d_2}$ and keep $V=V$ as the nuisance variable. Solve
for the old variables: from $F=\frac{U d_2}{V d_1}$ we get $u=\frac{d_1}{d_2}F\,v$ and
$v=v$. The Jacobian of $(u,v)$ with respect to $(F,v)$ is

$$
J = \begin{pmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial F} & \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial v} \\[8pt]
\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial F} & \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial v}
\end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{d_1}{d_2}v & \dfrac{d_1}{d_2}F \\[8pt] 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad |\det J| = \frac{d_1}{d_2}v,
$$

(the determinant is the product of the diagonal because the lower-left entry is $0$). So
$f_{F,V}(f,v)=f_{U,V}\bigl(\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}fv,\,v\bigr)\cdot\frac{d_1}{d_2}v$. Substitute
$u=\frac{d_1}{d_2}fv$ (so $u^{d_1/2-1}=(\frac{d_1}{d_2}f)^{d_1/2-1}v^{d_1/2-1}$ and the
exponential gains $e^{-\frac{d_1}{2 d_2}fv}$):

$$
f_{F,V}(f,v) = \frac{(\frac{d_1}{d_2}f)^{d_1/2-1}}{2^{d_1/2}\Gamma(d_1/2)\,2^{d_2/2}\Gamma(d_2/2)}
\cdot v^{d_1/2-1}\,v^{d_2/2-1}\,v
\cdot \exp\!\Bigl(-\tfrac{v}{2}\bigl(1+\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}f\bigr)\Bigr)\cdot\frac{d_1}{d_2}.
$$

Collect the powers of $v$: $v^{d_1/2-1}v^{d_2/2-1}v = v^{(d_1+d_2)/2-1}$. Pull every factor
free of $v$ to the front and integrate over $v\in(0,\infty)$:

$$
f_F(f) = \frac{(\frac{d_1}{d_2})^{d_1/2}\,f^{d_1/2-1}}{2^{(d_1+d_2)/2}\Gamma(d_1/2)\Gamma(d_2/2)}
\int_0^\infty v^{(d_1+d_2)/2-1}\,
\exp\!\Bigl(-\tfrac{v}{2}\bigl(1+\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}f\bigr)\Bigr)dv,
$$ {#eq-f-integral}

where the leading $\frac{d_1}{d_2}$ merged with $(\frac{d_1}{d_2}f)^{d_1/2-1}$ to give
$(\frac{d_1}{d_2})^{d_1/2}f^{d_1/2-1}$. The integral is the **Gamma integral**
$\int_0^\infty v^{a-1}e^{-bv}dv=\Gamma(a)/b^a$ (imported by name from [INFR-C2]/[PROB-15]),
with $a=\frac{d_1+d_2}{2}$ and $b=\frac12(1+\frac{d_1}{d_2}f)$:

$$
\int_0^\infty v^{(d_1+d_2)/2-1}e^{-bv}dv
= \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\bigr)}{b^{(d_1+d_2)/2}}
= \Gamma\!\Bigl(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\Bigr)\Bigl[\tfrac12\bigl(1+\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}f\bigr)\Bigr]^{-(d_1+d_2)/2}.
$$

The factor $[\tfrac12]^{-(d_1+d_2)/2}=2^{(d_1+d_2)/2}$ cancels the $2^{(d_1+d_2)/2}$ in the
denominator of @eq-f-integral. Assembling, and writing $B(\tfrac{d_1}{2},\tfrac{d_2}{2})
=\frac{\Gamma(d_1/2)\Gamma(d_2/2)}{\Gamma((d_1+d_2)/2)}$ for the Beta function:

::: {#thm-f-density}
**Theorem (F density).** The $F_{d_1,d_2}$ distribution has density, for $f>0$,
$$
f_{F_{d_1,d_2}}(f)
= \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\bigr)}{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1}{2}\bigr)\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_2}{2}\bigr)}
\Bigl(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\Bigr)^{d_1/2}
\frac{f^{\,d_1/2-1}}{\bigl(1+\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}f\bigr)^{(d_1+d_2)/2}}.
$$
:::

This is a positive, right-skewed density on $(0,\infty)$, with a peak below $1$ for small
degrees of freedom and concentrating onto $1$ as both $d_1,d_2\to\infty$ (the two mean
squares each converge to the true variance, so their ratio $\to1$). @fig-f-family draws it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-f-family
#| fig-cap: 'The $F_{d_1,d_2}$ family (@thm-f-density): the distribution of a ratio of two independent mean squares, each centered near 1. Under the null the ratio sits near 1; the right tail is the rejection region. As both degrees of freedom grow the density concentrates onto 1 (more data pins down each variance, so their ratio must agree). The asymmetry is essential: a variance ratio cannot go negative, so $F$ lives on $(0,\infty)$ and is right-skewed.'
f = np.linspace(0.001, 5, 600)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for (d1, d2), c in zip([(2, 8), (5, 10), (10, 30), (30, 100)],
                       [NULL, ACCENT, GREY, DATA]):
    ax.plot(f, stats.f.pdf(f, d1, d2), color=c, label=fr"$F_{{{d1},{d2}}}$")
ax.axvline(1.0, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.4)
ax.text(1.02, 0.9, "$F=1$", color=GREY, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlim(0, 5); ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05); ax.set_xlabel("$F$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title(r"The $F_{d_1,d_2}$ family concentrates on 1")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Comparing two variances {#sec-f-var}

The direct use: test $H_0:\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2$ for two independent Normal samples. From
[INFR-C2], $(n_1-1)s_1^2/\sigma_1^2\sim\chi^2_{n_1-1}$ and
$(n_2-1)s_2^2/\sigma_2^2\sim\chi^2_{n_2-1}$ independently. Form the ratio of mean squares;
under $H_0$ the $\sigma^2$'s are equal and cancel:

$$
F = \frac{s_1^2/\sigma_1^2}{s_2^2/\sigma_2^2}
\;\stackrel{H_0}{=}\; \frac{s_1^2}{s_2^2}
\;=\; \frac{\bigl[(n_1-1)s_1^2/\sigma_1^2\bigr]/(n_1-1)}{\bigl[(n_2-1)s_2^2/\sigma_2^2\bigr]/(n_2-1)}
\;\sim\; F_{\,n_1-1,\,n_2-1}.
$$ {#eq-f-var-test}

Each bracketed quantity is a chi-square over its degrees of freedom — exactly @def-f. We
reject when $F$ is far from $1$ in either tail of $F_{n_1-1,n_2-1}$.

## The F-test for nested models {#sec-f-nested}

This is the F-test that matters, and the reason the track routes it through Inference: it is
the engine **Regression** uses to ask *does adding these predictors significantly improve the
fit?* We state the general principle here; Regression applies it to specific linear models.

Two models are **nested** when the smaller (the *reduced* model, with $p_0$ parameters) is a
special case of the larger (the *full* model, with $p_1>p_0$) — obtained by forcing some
parameters to zero. Fit both by least squares and record the **residual sum of squares**
$\mathrm{RSS}_0\ge\mathrm{RSS}_1$ (the full model fits at least as well, so its residual sum
of squares is no larger). The F-statistic for $H_0:$ *the extra parameters are all zero* is

$$
F = \frac{(\mathrm{RSS}_0-\mathrm{RSS}_1)/(p_1-p_0)}{\mathrm{RSS}_1/(n-p_1)}.
$$ {#eq-f-nested}

Read it as @def-f. Under $H_0$ and Normal errors, $\mathrm{RSS}_1/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n-p_1}$
and the *improvement* $(\mathrm{RSS}_0-\mathrm{RSS}_1)/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{p_1-p_0}$
independently — the same orthogonality argument as [INFR-C2]'s mean/variance split, now
between the subspace the extra predictors span and the residual subspace. The $\sigma^2$
cancels in the ratio. The numerator is the *improvement per added parameter*; the
denominator is the *leftover noise per residual degree of freedom*. If the extra parameters
are truly zero, the improvement is just noise and $F\approx1$; if they capture real signal,
the improvement outpaces the noise and $F\gg1$. We flag the chi-square decompositions
themselves as the property of Normal-error least squares that Regression establishes in
full; here we have shown *why the ratio is an F* given them.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why a ratio? Why not just test whether $\\mathrm{RSS}_0-\\mathrm{RSS}_1$ is large?"*
Because "large" has no meaning without a scale, and the only honest scale is the noise level
$\sigma^2$ — which is **unknown**. The denominator $\mathrm{RSS}_1/(n-p_1)$ is precisely the
estimate of $\sigma^2$ from the full model's residuals. Taking the ratio makes $\sigma^2$
cancel, leaving a statistic whose null distribution ($F$) depends only on the known degrees
of freedom — a **pivot**, again. It is the exact analogue of why the $t$ divides by $s$
rather than by the unknown $\sigma$: when the noise scale is unknown, you build a ratio that
self-calibrates. This is also why, for testing a *single* added parameter, the F-statistic
equals the **square of the t-statistic** for that coefficient: $F_{1,d}=t_d^2$, which you
can verify from the definitions ($t_d=Z/\sqrt{\chi^2_d/d}$, so $t_d^2=Z^2/(\chi^2_d/d)
=\chi^2_1/(\chi^2_d/d)=F_{1,d}$).
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run three of the four on real, messy data, with the rejection region drawn each time.

## Example 1 — a two-sample t-test on reaction times {#sec-ex-ttest}

Two groups of participants perform a task; one group had a full night's sleep, the other was
sleep-restricted. We ask whether mean reaction time (ms) differs. The variances look
different, so — per the [INFR-C2] default — we use **Welch**, and we draw the result on the
$t_\nu$ null curve.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-ttest
#| fig-cap: 'Welch two-sample t-test, rested vs. sleep-restricted reaction times. **Left:** the two groups (jittered) with group means; the restricted group is slower and more variable. **Right:** the observed $t$ on its $t_\nu$ null curve, with the two-sided 5% rejection region shaded red and the observed statistic in green. The statistic lands deep in the right tail, so we reject $H_0:\mu_1=\mu_2$: the groups genuinely differ. The p-value is the green tail area (doubled).'
rested = np.array([242, 251, 238, 249, 255, 233, 247, 244, 240, 252, 236, 248.])
restr  = np.array([268, 295, 251, 312, 277, 259, 304, 283, 271, 330.])
n1, n2 = len(rested), len(restr)
m1, m2 = rested.mean(), restr.mean()
v1, v2 = rested.var(ddof=1), restr.var(ddof=1)
se = np.sqrt(v1/n1 + v2/n2)
tobs = (m1 - m2) / se
nu = (v1/n1 + v2/n2)**2 / ((v1/n1)**2/(n1-1) + (v2/n2)**2/(n2-1))
pval = 2 * stats.t.cdf(-abs(tobs), df=nu)
tc = stats.t.ppf(0.975, df=nu)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.scatter(rng.normal(0, 0.03, n1), rested, color=DATA, s=35, zorder=3, label=f"rested (s={np.sqrt(v1):.0f})")
axL.scatter(rng.normal(1, 0.03, n2), restr, color=NULL, s=35, zorder=3, label=f"restricted (s={np.sqrt(v2):.0f})")
axL.hlines([m1, m2], [-0.2, 0.8], [0.2, 1.2], color=[DATA, NULL], lw=2)
axL.set_xlim(-0.5, 1.5); axL.set_xticks([0, 1]); axL.set_xticklabels(["rested", "restricted"])
axL.set_ylabel("reaction time (ms)"); axL.set_title("Two groups, unequal spread")
axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)

x = np.linspace(-6, 6, 600)
axR.plot(x, stats.t.pdf(x, df=nu), color=DATA, lw=2)
xr = x[np.abs(x) >= tc]
axR.fill_between(xr, stats.t.pdf(xr, df=nu), color=NULL, alpha=0.30, label="5% rejection region")
xp = x[np.abs(x) >= abs(tobs)]
axR.fill_between(xp, stats.t.pdf(xp, df=nu), color=ACCENT, alpha=0.5, hatch="///")
axR.axvline(tobs, color=ACCENT, lw=2)
axR.text(tobs - 0.2, 0.30, fr"$t_{{\mathrm{{obs}}}}={tobs:.2f}$", color=ACCENT, ha="right", fontsize=9)
axR.set_xlabel("$t$"); axR.set_ylabel("density under $H_0$")
axR.set_title(fr"$t_{{\nu={nu:.1f}}}$ null; two-sided p={pval:.1e}")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"means: rested={m1:.1f}, restricted={m2:.1f};  diff={m1-m2:.1f} ms")
print(f"Welch t = {tobs:.3f},  nu = {nu:.2f},  two-sided p = {pval:.2e}")
print(f"reject at 0.05? {'yes' if abs(tobs) > tc else 'no'} (|t|={abs(tobs):.2f} vs t_crit={tc:.2f})")
```

The restricted group averages about $`{python} f"{m2-m1:.0f}"`$ ms slower. The Welch
statistic is $t=`{python} f"{tobs:.2f}"`$ on $\nu=`{python} f"{nu:.1f}"`$ degrees of
freedom, with two-sided $p=`{python} f"{pval:.1e}"`$ — far below $0.05$, so the difference is
distinguishable from noise. By the duality, a $95\%$ Welch confidence interval for the
difference would exclude zero, which is the same conclusion read as an interval.

## Example 2 — a chi-square test of independence {#sec-ex-chisq}

Does treatment outcome depend on which of three clinics administered it? We have a
$3\times2$ table (clinic $\times$ {improved, not improved}) and test independence with
@eq-chisq-indep on $(r-1)(c-1)=2$ degrees of freedom.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-chisq
#| fig-cap: 'Chi-square test of independence, clinic vs. outcome ($3\times2$ table). **Left:** observed counts (bars) against the expected counts under independence (black ticks), $E_{ij}=R_iC_j/n$. Clinic C improves more often than independence predicts; clinic A less. **Right:** the observed $X^2$ on its $\chi^2_2$ null curve with the 5% right-tail rejection region. The statistic exceeds the critical value, so outcome is **not** independent of clinic.'
# rows = clinics A,B,C ; cols = improved, not improved
O = np.array([[34, 46],
              [42, 38],
              [58, 22]], dtype=float)
R = O.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)
C = O.sum(axis=0, keepdims=True)
N = O.sum()
E = R @ C / N
X2 = np.sum((O - E)**2 / E)
r, c = O.shape
df = (r - 1) * (c - 1)
pval = stats.chi2.sf(X2, df)
crit = stats.chi2.ppf(0.95, df)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
clinics = ["A", "B", "C"]
xpos = np.arange(r)
axL.bar(xpos - 0.2, O[:, 0], width=0.38, color=ACCENT, label="improved (obs)")
axL.bar(xpos + 0.2, O[:, 1], width=0.38, color=GREY, label="not improved (obs)")
for i in range(r):
    axL.hlines(E[i, 0], i - 0.39, i - 0.01, color="black", lw=2)
    axL.hlines(E[i, 1], i + 0.01, i + 0.39, color="black", lw=2)
axL.plot([], [], color="black", lw=2, label="expected under indep.")
axL.set_xticks(xpos); axL.set_xticklabels([f"clinic {x}" for x in clinics])
axL.set_ylabel("count"); axL.set_title("Observed vs. expected counts")
axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)

q = np.linspace(0.01, max(18, X2 + 2), 500)
axR.plot(q, stats.chi2.pdf(q, df), color=DATA, lw=2)
qr = q[q >= crit]
axR.fill_between(qr, stats.chi2.pdf(qr, df), color=NULL, alpha=0.30,
                 label=fr"5% region $\chi^2\geq{crit:.2f}$")
axR.axvline(X2, color=ACCENT, lw=2)
axR.text(X2 + 0.2, 0.12, fr"$X^2={X2:.1f}$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=9)
axR.set_xlabel(r"$X^2$"); axR.set_ylabel("density under $H_0$")
axR.set_title(fr"$\chi^2_{{{df}}}$ null; p={pval:.4f}")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print("Expected counts E_ij = R_i C_j / n:")
print(np.round(E, 2))
print(f"X^2 = sum (O-E)^2/E = {X2:.3f} on df=(r-1)(c-1)={df}")
print(f"p = {pval:.4f};  reject at 0.05? {'yes' if X2 > crit else 'no'} (crit={crit:.2f})")
```

The expected counts assume each clinic improves at the *overall* rate; the observed counts
deviate, with clinic C improving more and A less than independence predicts. The statistic
$X^2=`{python} f"{X2:.1f}"`$ on $2$ degrees of freedom gives $p=`{python} f"{pval:.4f}"`$ —
below $0.05$, so we reject independence: outcome and clinic are associated. (What the test
does *not* tell you is *why* — confounding by case mix, clinic quality, or referral patterns
are all consistent with this table. A significant chi-square is the beginning of an
investigation, not its end.)

## Example 3 — an F-test for a nested model {#sec-ex-f}

Does adding a quadratic term to a linear fit significantly improve it? The reduced model is
$y=\beta_0+\beta_1x+\varepsilon$ ($p_0=2$); the full model adds $\beta_2x^2$ ($p_1=3$). We
fit both, form @eq-f-nested, and test $H_0:\beta_2=0$ on $F_{1,\,n-3}$. We do this on data
that genuinely curve, so the test *should* reject — an honest example where the workhorse
does its job.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-f
#| fig-cap: 'F-test for a nested model: is the quadratic term needed? **Left:** data with a real downward curvature, the linear fit (red) systematically missing it, and the quadratic fit (green) tracking it. **Right:** the observed $F$ on its $F_{1,n-3}$ null curve with the 5% right-tail rejection region. $F$ lands far out, so we reject $H_0:\beta_2=0$ — the quadratic term captures real signal, not noise.'
nobs = 40
xv = np.linspace(0, 10, nobs)
yv = 3 + 2.2*xv - 0.28*xv**2 + rng.normal(0, 1.6, nobs)

def fit_poly(x, y, deg):
    Xmat = np.vander(x, deg + 1, increasing=True)
    coef, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(Xmat, y, rcond=None)
    resid = y - Xmat @ coef
    return coef, np.sum(resid**2)

c0, rss0 = fit_poly(xv, yv, 1)   # linear, p0=2
c1, rss1 = fit_poly(xv, yv, 2)   # quadratic, p1=3
p0, p1 = 2, 3
Fobs = ((rss0 - rss1) / (p1 - p0)) / (rss1 / (nobs - p1))
dfn, dfd = p1 - p0, nobs - p1
pval = stats.f.sf(Fobs, dfn, dfd)
crit = stats.f.ppf(0.95, dfn, dfd)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 200)
axL.scatter(xv, yv, color=DATA, s=28, zorder=3, label="data")
axL.plot(xs, c0[0] + c0[1]*xs, color=NULL, lw=2, label="linear (reduced)")
axL.plot(xs, c1[0] + c1[1]*xs + c1[2]*xs**2, color=ACCENT, lw=2, label="quadratic (full)")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$"); axL.set_title("Reduced vs. full model")
axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)

ff = np.linspace(0.001, max(8, Fobs + 2), 500)
axR.plot(ff, stats.f.pdf(ff, dfn, dfd), color=DATA, lw=2)
fr_ = ff[ff >= crit]
axR.fill_between(fr_, stats.f.pdf(fr_, dfn, dfd), color=NULL, alpha=0.30,
                 label=fr"5% region $F\geq{crit:.2f}$")
axR.axvline(Fobs, color=ACCENT, lw=2)
axR.text(min(Fobs, 7.0) + 0.1, 0.4, fr"$F={Fobs:.1f}$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=9)
axR.set_xlim(0, max(8, crit + 2)); axR.set_xlabel("$F$"); axR.set_ylabel("density under $H_0$")
axR.set_title(fr"$F_{{{dfn},{dfd}}}$ null; p={pval:.2e}")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"RSS reduced (linear)   = {rss0:.2f}")
print(f"RSS full   (quadratic) = {rss1:.2f}")
print(f"F = [(RSS0-RSS1)/{dfn}] / [RSS1/{dfd}] = {Fobs:.3f} on F({dfn},{dfd})")
print(f"p = {pval:.2e};  also note t^2 check: sqrt(F) = {np.sqrt(Fobs):.3f} = |t| for beta2")
```

The residual sum of squares drops from $`{python} f"{rss0:.1f}"`$ to
$`{python} f"{rss1:.1f}"`$ when the quadratic term is added; the F-statistic
$`{python} f"{Fobs:.1f}"`$ on $F_{1,`{python} f"{dfd}"`}$ gives $p=`{python} f"{pval:.1e}"`$.
The improvement vastly outpaces the residual noise, so $\beta_2\ne0$: the curvature is real.
Note the printed check $\sqrt F=|t|$ — for this single-parameter comparison the F-test and
the $t$-test of $\beta_2=0$ are the identical test, exactly as the @sec-f-nested callout
promised.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

Each workhorse has a null distribution that is *exact* or *approximate* only under specific
assumptions. The practitioner's skill is knowing which test survives which violation. This
is the honest boundary.

## When the chi-square count test breaks: sparse cells {#sec-sparse}

The chi-square null distribution @eq-gof-null is an **asymptotic** result: it comes from the
CLT approximating each count by a Normal. That approximation needs the expected counts $E_i$
to be reasonably large. When some $E_i$ are tiny, the count is still lumpy and discrete — it
cannot pretend to be a smooth Normal — and the *true* distribution of $X^2$ no longer
matches the $\chi^2_{k-1}$ curve we compare it against. The honest way to see the failure is
to look at the **whole null distribution**, not just the headline Type I rate at $\alpha=
0.05$. @fig-sparse simulates that null distribution directly and exposes a subtlety the
$E_i\ge5$ folklore hides: the chi-square test stays *roughly* calibrated at the coarse $0.05$
cutoff even with tiny cells (the central body of the simulated null lines up with $\chi^2_5$),
but its **tail** is wrong — the simulated null puts the wrong probability past the $1\%$
cutoff — and the tail is exactly where the small p-values that drive discoveries live. The
two panels (sparse vs. ample) and the printed Type I rates at the $5\%$ and $1\%$ cutoffs make
both faces of this measurable, not asserted.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sparse
#| fig-cap: 'The edge of applicability for the chi-square count test, shown honestly. Both panels simulate $X^2$ under the **null** for a skewed 6-cell model with one rare category. **Left ($n=20$, tiny expected counts):** the simulated null distribution of $X^2$ (blue histogram) is discrete and lumpy and does **not** match the $\chi^2_5$ density (red) — especially in the right tail, so a small ($p<0.01$) p-value read off the $\chi^2$ curve is not trustworthy. **Right ($n=300$, ample counts):** the histogram now tracks $\chi^2_5$ closely. The printed numbers show the subtlety: the Type I rate at the 5% cutoff stays near 0.05 even when sparse, but the rate at the **1% cutoff** is noticeably inflated when counts are tiny — the body is fine, the tail is not.'
probs = np.array([0.5, 0.2, 0.12, 0.08, 0.06, 0.04])
k = len(probs)
df = k - 1
B = 60000

def null_X2(n):
    E = n * probs
    counts = rng.multinomial(n, probs, size=B)
    return np.sum((counts - E)**2 / E, axis=1)

X2_small = null_X2(20)
X2_big   = null_X2(300)
qq = np.linspace(0.01, 20, 400)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8), sharey=True)
axL.hist(X2_small, bins=120, range=(0, 20), density=True, color=DATA, alpha=0.55,
         label="null $X^2$ ($n=20$)")
axL.plot(qq, stats.chi2.pdf(qq, df), color=NULL, lw=2, label=fr"$\chi^2_{{{df}}}$ density")
axL.set_xlim(0, 20); axL.set_xlabel(r"$X^2$"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("Sparse: tail mismatch"); axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)

axR.hist(X2_big, bins=120, range=(0, 20), density=True, color=DATA, alpha=0.55,
         label="null $X^2$ ($n=300$)")
axR.plot(qq, stats.chi2.pdf(qq, df), color=NULL, lw=2, label=fr"$\chi^2_{{{df}}}$ density")
axR.set_xlim(0, 20); axR.set_xlabel(r"$X^2$")
axR.set_title("Ample: matches"); axR.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()

for n, X2v in [(20, X2_small), (300, X2_big)]:
    minE = n * probs.min()
    t05 = np.mean(X2v > stats.chi2.ppf(0.95, df))
    t01 = np.mean(X2v > stats.chi2.ppf(0.99, df))
    print(f"n={n:>3} (min E={minE:4.1f}): Type I @5% = {t05:.3f} (target .05),  "
          f"@1% = {t01:.3f} (target .01)")
```

The lesson is sharper than the usual folklore. The "$E_i\ge5$ everywhere" rule is real, but
the danger is not a wildly inflated 5% Type I rate — it is that the **tail probabilities are
wrong**, so the *small* p-values you would actually act on ($p<0.01$) are unreliable when
counts are sparse, even though the test looks calibrated at the coarse 5% level.

**When you reach for something else:** with sparse counts use **Fisher's exact test** (which
computes the exact hypergeometric p-value rather than a Normal approximation), or merge
sparse categories, or use a Monte-Carlo / permutation p-value ([INFR-E3]) — all of which
replace the mismatched $\chi^2$ curve with the true (or simulated) null distribution.

## When the variance and F tests break: non-normality {#sec-nonnormal-f}

The chi-square test for a variance (@sec-var-test) and the F-test for comparing variances
(@sec-f-var) are the **least robust** workhorses, because they rest entirely on the data
being Normal — not just on $\bar X$ being approximately Normal (which the CLT delivers for
the $z$/$t$ tests), but on the *whole distribution* being Normal, since their statistics are
sums of *squared* deviations whose distribution is set by the fourth moment (the kurtosis).
The CLT does **not** rescue them. @fig-f-robustness shows the damage.

```{python}
#| label: fig-f-robustness
#| fig-cap: "Robustness, compared honestly. Simulating under the null (equal variances / equal means), we record the actual Type I rate of three nominal-5% tests as the data move from Normal to heavy-tailed ($t_3$) to skewed (exponential). The **Welch t-test** for means (green) stays near 0.05 throughout — it inherits the CLT's protection. The **F-test for variances** (red) is wrecked: under heavy tails it rejects far more than 5% of the time even though the variances are equal, because its statistic depends on the tail shape the Normal assumption fixed. Robustness is a property of the *statistic*, not of testing in general."
def welch_t1(dist, n=15, B=8000):
    cnt = 0
    for _ in range(B):
        a = dist(n); b = dist(n)
        v1, v2 = a.var(ddof=1), b.var(ddof=1)
        se = np.sqrt(v1/n + v2/n)
        t = (a.mean() - b.mean()) / se
        nu = (v1/n + v2/n)**2 / ((v1/n)**2/(n-1) + (v2/n)**2/(n-1))
        cnt += abs(t) > stats.t.ppf(0.975, nu)
    return cnt / B

def f_var1(dist, n=15, B=8000):
    crit_hi = stats.f.ppf(0.975, n-1, n-1)
    crit_lo = stats.f.ppf(0.025, n-1, n-1)
    cnt = 0
    for _ in range(B):
        a = dist(n); b = dist(n)
        F = a.var(ddof=1) / b.var(ddof=1)
        cnt += (F > crit_hi) or (F < crit_lo)
    return cnt / B

dists = {
    "Normal":      lambda n: rng.standard_normal(n),
    "heavy ($t_3$)": lambda n: rng.standard_t(3, n),
    "skewed (exp)":  lambda n: rng.exponential(1.0, n) - 1.0,
}
welch_rates = [welch_t1(d) for d in dists.values()]
fvar_rates  = [f_var1(d) for d in dists.values()]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
xp = np.arange(len(dists))
ax.bar(xp - 0.2, welch_rates, width=0.38, color=ACCENT, label="Welch t (means)")
ax.bar(xp + 0.2, fvar_rates, width=0.38, color=NULL, label="F-test (variances)")
ax.axhline(0.05, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.5, label="nominal 0.05")
ax.set_xticks(xp); ax.set_xticklabels(list(dists.keys()))
ax.set_ylabel("actual Type I rate (nominal 5%)")
ax.set_title("The F-for-variances test is not robust; the t-for-means is")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
for name, w, f in zip(dists.keys(), welch_rates, fvar_rates):
    print(f"{name:>14}: Welch-t Type I = {w:.3f}   F-variance Type I = {f:.3f}")
```

**When you reach for something else:** to compare spreads robustly, use **Levene's** or the
**Brown–Forsythe** test (which apply an ANOVA F to absolute deviations from the median,
draining away the dependence on Normality), or a **bootstrap** ([INFR-E1]) of the variance
ratio. Crucially, this is also why you should **not** run an F-test for equal variances as a
preliminary screen before choosing pooled vs. Welch $t$ (the trap [INFR-C2] flagged): the
screen is itself unreliable on non-Normal data, and the two-stage procedure distorts the
final test's level. Just use Welch.

## When the t-test strains: skew and dependence {#sec-t-strain}

The $z$ and $t$ tests for means are the *most* robust workhorses, because they standardize
$\bar X$, which the CLT pushes toward Normal. But two things still break them. **Heavy skew
at small $n$** undercovers exactly as [INFR-C2]'s coverage-breakdown figure showed — the test
inherits the same flaw, since a test is an interval read backward. And **dependence** is
fatal to *all* of them: every null distribution here assumed i.i.d. (or independent
between-group) data. If observations are correlated — repeated measures on the same subject,
time series, clustered sampling — the true standard error is larger (or smaller) than the
i.i.d. formula, the statistic is mis-standardized, and the p-value is meaningless. No amount
of choosing the "right" curve fixes a wrong denominator.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Dependence breaks every test in this article, silently.** The $z$, $t$, $\chi^2$, and F
all derive their null distributions assuming independent observations. Positively correlated
data (the common case: repeated measures, spatial or temporal clustering) make the effective
sample size *smaller* than $n$, so the real standard error is *larger* than $\sigma/\sqrt n$
— the statistic is inflated, and the test rejects far more than $\alpha$ of the time under
the null. The danger is that the software prints a clean p-value regardless; nothing warns
you. The fixes live outside this article: paired/within-subject designs (reduce to
independent differences, as the [INFR-C2] paired example did), mixed-effects models,
cluster-robust standard errors, or block/permutation methods that respect the dependence
structure ([INFR-E3]). **Always ask how the data were collected before trusting any of these
four tests.**
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Given a one- or two-sample question, how do you pick the test — and when is none of these
right?"* The decision you should be able to recite:

- **Mean(s), scale unknown, data plausibly Normal or $n$ moderate:** $t$-test (one- or
  two-sample; Welch by default for two). This is the everyday answer and the most robust.
- **Mean(s), scale genuinely known** (rare — calibrated instruments, simulation with fixed
  noise): $z$-test. Otherwise the $t$.
- **Counts against a model / association in a table:** chi-square goodness-of-fit or
  independence — but check $E_i\gtrsim5$; if sparse, Fisher's exact or a permutation test.
- **A variance, or comparing two variances:** chi-square / F — but *only* if you trust
  Normality, which you usually should not; prefer Levene or a bootstrap.
- **Comparing nested models:** the F-test (the Regression workhorse), which reduces to a
  $t$-test for a single added parameter.
- **None of these when:** the data are dependent (use a model that encodes the dependence),
  badly non-Normal at small $n$ (use the **bootstrap** [INFR-E1] or a **permutation test**
  [INFR-E3]), or the quantity of interest is not a mean/variance/count (the median, a
  quantile, a correlation — again bootstrap). The deepest point: these four are the cases
  where the null distribution is a *named curve you can look up*. When it is not, you
  *simulate* the null distribution — which is exactly what the bootstrap and permutation tests
  do, and why they close this movement.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*]**, which built the
decision frame — null, alternative, $\alpha$, power, the p-value, and the test/interval
duality — and from **[INFR-C2 *the t-Distribution*]**, which built the chi-square and $t$
distributions this article turned into decisions. Here we instantiated the logic as the four
workhorses, unified them as *standardized discrepancies*, and derived the F-distribution as a
ratio of independent chi-squares. From here:

- **[INFR-D3 *The Asymptotic Trinity*]** generalizes all of this. The chi-square tests here
  are the simplest cases of the **likelihood-ratio**, **Wald**, and **score** tests, whose
  null distribution is $\chi^2$ by **Wilks' theorem** — the asymptotic engine that tests
  *any* hypothesis about an MLE-fit model, not just means and counts.
- **[INFR-D4 *Power and Sample Size*]** asks the design question: given that we will run one
  of these tests, how large a sample do we need for it to actually detect an effect? The
  power of the $z$/$t$ test is derived there from the non-null distribution of the statistic.
- **[INFR-D5 *Multiple Comparisons*]** asks what happens when you run *many* of these tests
  at once — the false-discovery problem that the genomics and A/B-testing worlds cannot avoid.
- **[INFR-E3 *Permutation Tests*]** is the assumption-light alternative this article kept
  pointing to: when the data are non-Normal, sparse, or you want a test for a statistic with
  no named null distribution, you *build* the null distribution by relabeling rather than
  looking it up.

This article is consumed directly by **Regression**: the **F-test for nested models**
(@sec-f-nested) is exactly how Regression tests whether a group of predictors belongs in a
model, the $t$-test of @sec-t is how it tests a single coefficient, and the $F=t^2$ identity
ties the two together. The chi-square tests reappear there as the deviance tests for
generalized linear models.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The original goodness-of-fit paper.** K. Pearson, "On the criterion that a given system
  of deviations from the probable…," *Philosophical Magazine* 50 (1900) — the introduction of
  $X^2=\sum(O-E)^2/E$. Pearson initially miscounted the degrees of freedom for the
  independence case; R. A. Fisher corrected it to $(r-1)(c-1)$ in the 1920s, the
  constraint-counting derivation of @sec-independence.
- **The F-distribution.** Named by G. W. Snedecor for Fisher, who introduced the variance
  ratio for the analysis of variance in *Statistical Methods for Research Workers* (1925) —
  the F-test for nested models is ANOVA's beating heart, and Regression's.
- **Exact alternatives.** R. A. Fisher, *The Design of Experiments* (1935), Ch. 2, on the
  exact test (the "lady tasting tea") — the exact and permutation logic that replaces the
  chi-square approximation when counts are sparse, developed fully in [INFR-E3].
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *What if the hypothesis is about something other
  than a mean, variance, or count?* — the general MLE-based trinity, [INFR-D3]. *How do I
  test many groups' means at once rather than two?* — the one-way ANOVA F-test, the
  multi-group generalization of @sec-f-nested, which Regression builds as a model with a
  categorical predictor. *What is the most powerful test I could possibly use?* — the
  **Neyman–Pearson lemma**, [INFR-D3], which explains *why* likelihood-ratio statistics (and
  hence these as their special cases) are optimal.
